title: Kiss all the Time. Disco, Occasionally
notes:
I think this album is a really great example of individually good songs making a great album. This is the kind of project that I really enjoy because of the artistry. There is a cohesive story about someone falling in love and perhaps not getting his happy ending, finishing with advice to seemingly themself. Whether this is a self insert or not, I think everyone can relate to the emotions in the album. Mostly up-beat to otherwise harrowing lyrics ("Do I let you down? Holding, Holding out, hoping that you will love me now" -*Season 2 Weight Loss*) keeps it replayable and not too intense. And all the while there is a quiet musicianship that I think might be lost to a lot of casual listeners. What I mean by that is that none of the music is terribly complex, and no one is doing any crazy riffs (except whoever had the disgusting guitar riff in *Pop*), but there is a lot of intentionality in the small things. The basses in *Coming Up Roses* being slightly ahead of the beat tp keep you on your toes, his trademark harmony style, and the vocal textures he displays are some examples.

Like I said, a lot of songs on this record are a little mundane and frankly forgettable sometimes. Having said this, no song on the album is below a 7 for me. *Taste Back*, *Waiting Game*, and *Carla's Songs" are some of these songs for me, individually 8's imo, ballads that I will definitely listen to if I am in the mood. But if I was asked right after the album to name all the songs and hum a bar, those two would be difficult. Calling them forgettable is a bit harsh, but I think it is both on purpose and a product of Harry's vocal texture; the wispy, dreamlike qualities can blend together when the song is a slower ballad. Which works really well on the record in my opinion. Having the melancholy bits of the love story be deep and a little mundane is accurate to the emotions. Looking back on similar periods of life, I tend to also blur memories and make them forgettable (probably to not return to the emotions I was feeling at the time).

Stand outs for me were definitely *Coming Up Roses* and *American Girls*. I was incredibly impressed at how well written the strings are in *Coming Up Roses*. So much so that I would ask why we haven't had more of them in other Harry ballads. It reminds me a bit of *Mia and Sebastien's Theme*, reminicent of a lullaby, day-dream of what this make believe romance. The naive hope of a honeymoon period. The bridge is one of the most evocative pieces of art I have experienced in a while. *American Girls* is more classic Harry Styles. A standard chord progression with small harmonies, good lyrics, and really tastefully produced synth. Extremely catchy and quite enjoyable on my second and third and fourth listens and maybe on my fifth or sixth that are coming up. As always, I wish there was more funk and groove in his albums. I think *Music for a Sushi Restaurant* off Harry's House and *Late Night Talking* off Fine Line are personally some of my favorite songs of his. I think he has more of that genre to give, but that is really his peroggative. Overally, a great piece of art that I can definitely listen to a hundred times.
